title: Photonic Analog Quantum Simulation of (1+1)-Dimensional $U(1)$ Lattice Gauge Theory with Dynamical Matter
authors: Nathan R. Gonzalez, Thea Budde, Klemen Kersic, Zia Steele, Alex H. Rubin, Joao C. Pinto Barros, Marina Radulaski, Marina Krstic Marinkovic
abstract: We propose a photonic scheme for analog quantum simulation of a $U(1)$ Lattice Gauge Theory (LGT) with dynamical matter based on the Jaynes-Cummings-Hubbard (JCH) model. Here, an array of interacting cavities in the strong-coupling regime of cavity Quantum Electrodynamics is mapped onto the alternating matter and gauge-field sites of the spin-1/2 Quantum Link Model. In contrast to other analog LGT quantum simulation methods, our approach implements the desired gauge-invariant dynamics through the hopping of polaritonic excitations among the array sites. The hopping is mapped to the gauge theory via precise tuning of polaritonic resonances in individual cavities. Using exact diagonalization, we show that the real-time evolution of the JCH model accurately replicates that of a Quantum Link Model. Finally, we discuss feasible routes to the beyond-classical simulation capability with scalable implementations in photonic and superconducting systems. This provides a novel route towards understanding the real-time dynamics of lattice gauge theories with matter in higher dimensions.
summary: This paper proposes using coupled cavity arrays in cavity QED to analog quantum simulate a 1D U(1) Lattice Gauge Theory. By engineering the Jaynes-Cummings-Hubbard model, the authors successfully map the system's dynamics onto the target gauge theory, confirming its gauge-invariant evolution. This establishes a novel, scalable platform for studying complex many-body physics beyond classical simulation capabilities.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of developing a quantum simulation platform to study the real-time dynamics of a (1+1)-Dimensional U(1) Lattice Gauge Theory (LGT) coupled to dynamical matter.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the dynamics of the target LGT can be accurately replicated by engineering the Jaynes-Cummings-Hubbard (JCH) model in a coupled cavity array, confirming its potential for simulating gauge-invariant physics.
method: The simulation relies on mapping the LGT onto the JCH model using resonant engineering of polaritonic excitations, followed by verifying the dynamics using exact diagonalization.
model_or_system: The physical system is an array of interacting cavities operating in the strong-coupling regime of Cavity Quantum Electrodynamics (QED). The underlying model is the JCH Hamiltonian, which is engineered to mimic the spin-1/2 Quantum Link Model (QLM) Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Gauge invariance (monitored via Gauss's law expectation value $\langle G_l angle$) and the confinement of the evolution to the physical subspace are key diagnostics.
important_parameters: The simulation requires operating in the strong-coupling regime ($g \gg J$) and precise tuning of cavity detunings ($\Delta_i$) to achieve resonant coupling.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the system can be effectively described by the JCH model and relies on deriving an effective Hamiltonian via second-order perturbation theory.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mapping of the U(1) gauge theory onto a bosonic chain and schematically depict the mapping between the JCH model and the spin-1/2 QLM in a coupled cavity array.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the LGT problem, details the mapping from the QLM to the JCH model using cavity QED principles, shows the successful replication of real-time dynamics via numerical simulation, and concludes by discussing scalable implementations in photonic and superconducting platforms.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it uses advanced cavity QED techniques (a core AMO topic) to tackle a fundamental problem in high-energy/condensed matter theory (LGTs), providing a concrete, experimentally viable quantum simulation blueprint.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The U(1) gauge theory can be mapped exactly onto a bosonic chain with 2L −1 sites. The bosonic occupations are uniquely mapped to a gauge-theory configuration depending on the site’s parity. The left/right pointing yellow arrows represent the polarization of the gauge field ⟨Sz l ⟩= ±1/2. A filled/empty circle indicates an occupied/empty fermionic site. An occupied bosonic matter site corresponds to the presence of a charge in the QLM, while the location of doubly occupied gauge field sites indicates the polarization of the gauge field.

Figure 2. Mapping between the JCH model and the spin-1/2 QLM. (a) Schematic of a coupled cavity array in the strong coupling regime (g ≫J), consisting of alternating matter (blue) and gauge (orange) sites, each composed of a cavity coupled to a two-level emitter with a coupling rate g. Photon hopping between cavities occurs at a rate J. Colors indicate varying cavity frequency ωe. (b) Polariton energy level structure for each cavity. The anharmonic splitting of the JC ladder is tuned to support the resonance conditions E2i 2,−= E2i−1 1,−+ E2i+1 1,− to be satisfied simultaneously for all i across the array.

Figure 3. Real-time dynamics for the JCH model on a chain of five coupled cavities compared to the spin-1/2 QLM. (a) Matter site and (b) gauge field JCH occupation mapped to QLM observables (charge density and electric flux, respectively) as described by Eqs. (8) and (9). (c) Absolute value of the expectation value for the Gauss’s law operator |⟨Gl⟩| for each matter site l and the two adjacent gauge sites, which remains close to zero for all times, confirming confinement to the physical subspace by the gauge protection term λ. Gauss’s law Gl is defined in Eq. (4). Projection onto the physical state for (d) gauge fields and (e) matter sites as described by the set of equations in Eq. (10)....

Figure 3 shows the real-time evolution comparison be- tween the full JCH model and spin-1/2 QLM for param- eters ωe/J = 500, ∆0/J = 111.1, g/J = 333.3, and λ/J = 40. The cavity modes are restricted to hold at most Nc = 3 photons to reduce the simulation runtime. To make comparisons, we map observables from one model to the other. Figure 4. The possible strength of the effective interaction t varies with the parameters. The color indicates the maximum value of t that can be engineered while keeping J perturba- tively small. This ensures gauge invariance at intermediate timescales. White indicates that there is no valid solution to the resonance condition for these parameters.

Spintronics.note: The paper uses the Jaynes-Cummings-Hubbard model in coupled cavities (Cavity QED) to simulate physics, which directly overlaps with the methodology and physical regime of cavity QED applied to spin/magnet systems.
